skill.name: adapt
skill.description: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Ensures consistent experience across varied environments.
skill.summary: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Ensures consistent experience across varied environments.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 31 potential issues, all evaluated as false positives. The 'external_commands' detections are CSS/HTML code examples (clamp(), display:none, srcset) incorrectly flagged. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' are common English words (desktop, mobile, context) wrongly matched. This is a pure design guidance skill with no executable code, network calls, or file operations.

content.user_title: Adapt Designs for Any Screen or Platform
content.value_statement: Struggling to make your design work across mobile, tablet, desktop, print, or email? This skill provides expert guidance on adapting designs for different contexts while maintaining consistency and usability.
actual_capabilities: Analyze source design context and identify adaptation challenges
Plan mobile, tablet, desktop, print, and email adaptation strategies
Recommend layout, interaction, and content adaptation techniques
Guide responsive implementation using CSS Grid, Flexbox, container queries
Suggest touch optimization and accessibility considerations
Provide verification and testing strategies across devices
limitations: Does not write actual code - provides guidance and recommendations only
Cannot preview or test designs on real devices
Does not have access to your specific design files or codebase
Does not generate complete implementation code

use_cases.title: Mobile Website Redesign
use_cases.description: Adapt an existing desktop website for mobile users with touch-friendly interactions and optimized layouts

use_cases.title: Cross-Platform App UI
use_cases.description: Ensure a web app works seamlessly across desktop browsers and mobile tablets with consistent UX
use_cases.target_user: UI/UX designers working on responsive applications

use_cases.title: Email Newsletter Design
use_cases.description: Convert a web-based announcement into a mobile-friendly email compatible with all major email clients

prompt_templates.title: Basic Mobile Adaptation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt this [component/page/design] for mobile devices. It was originally designed for [desktop/tablet]. What changes should I make to layout, navigation, and interactions?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user needing mobile design guidance

prompt_templates.title: Tablet and Desktop Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a mobile-first design and need to adapt it for tablet and desktop. Create an adaptation strategy that uses horizontal space effectively while maintaining consistency.
prompt_templates.scenario: Designer expanding from mobile to larger screens

prompt_templates.title: Print and Email Adaptation
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I adapt a web page design for [print/email]? What are the key differences in layout, content, and technical constraints I need to consider?

prompt_templates.title: Responsive Implementation Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Provide specific CSS techniques for implementing responsive design including breakpoints, container queries, fluid typography with clamp(), and touch target sizing.

output_examples.input: Help me adapt a complex data dashboard for mobile
output_examples.output: For mobile dashboards, prioritize: single-column layout, collapsible sections for secondary data, larger touch targets (44px minimum), sticky headers with key metrics, bottom navigation for quick access, progressive disclosure for complex charts. Consider hiding low-priority widgets entirely on mobile rather than stacking everything.

output_examples.input: What are best practices for tablet landscape orientation?
output_examples.output: Tablet landscape excels at master-detail patterns, side-by-side content panels, multi-column forms, and inline editing. Use two-column layouts rather than single or three-column. Support both touch and pointer input. Allow orientation-specific layouts while maintaining information architecture consistency.

best_practices: Start with mobile-first approach when possible, then progressively enhance for larger screens
Use content-driven breakpoints instead of arbitrary device widths
Test on real devices rather than relying solely on browser DevTools emulation
Maintain consistent information architecture across all breakpoints and platforms
anti_patterns: Hiding core functionality on mobile instead of making it work
Using different information architecture on mobile vs desktop
Breaking user expectations for each platform (e.g., hamburger menus on desktop)
Using generic breakpoints blindly without considering where the design actually breaks

faq.question: What is the difference between responsive and adaptive design?
faq.answer: Responsive design uses fluid layouts and CSS to adapt to any screen size. Adaptive design uses predefined layouts for specific device categories. Most modern sites use responsive techniques.

faq.question: What are the standard responsive breakpoints?
faq.answer: Common breakpoints are: mobile (320-767px), tablet (768-1023px), desktop (1024px+). However, content-driven breakpoints that match where your design breaks are more effective than device-based ones.

faq.question: How do I handle images for different screen sizes?
faq.answer: Use srcset and the picture element to serve different image sizes based on viewport. Also consider the format property for modern formats like WebP with fallbacks, and lazy loading for off-screen images.

faq.question: What touch target size should I use?
faq.answer: Use minimum 44x44px touch targets for all interactive elements. Increase spacing between targets to prevent accidental taps. Remove hover-dependent interactions since hover does not work on touch devices.

faq.question: How do I adapt navigation for mobile?
faq.answer: Use hamburger menus or bottom navigation bars on mobile. Keep navigation simple with fewer items. Ensure back buttons are available in the navigation flow. Consider sticky headers for context.

faq.question: Should I use a mobile-specific domain or subdomain?
faq.answer: No, responsive design with a single URL is preferred for SEO and user experience. Separate mobile sites (m.example.com) create maintenance overhead and can hurt search rankings.
